description: First global normative instrument on AI ethics, adopted by 193 member states in November 2021. Four core values, ten principles, and eleven policy action areas.

principles.name: Human Dignity and Human Rights
principles.definition: AI systems should be designed and operated to be compatible with the protection and promotion of human dignity and fundamental rights.
principles.description: The development and use of AI must respect, protect and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms and human dignity.

principles.name: Living in Peaceful, Just and Interconnected Societies
principles.definition: AI should promote peace, justice, and interconnectedness among peoples and nations.
principles.description: AI actors should promote peaceful coexistence, social justice, and interconnected societies.

principles.name: Ensuring Diversity and Inclusiveness
principles.definition: AI should respect, protect, and promote diversity and inclusiveness.
principles.description: Throughout the AI lifecycle, AI systems should promote social and cultural diversity, protect Indigenous peoples' rights, and prevent exclusion.

principles.name: Environment and Ecosystem Flourishing
principles.definition: AI actors should address environmental impact and promote sustainable development.
principles.description: AI systems should be designed and used sustainably, with assessed and reduced environmental impact throughout their lifecycle.

principles.name: Proportionality and Do No Harm
principles.definition: AI system deployment must be proportionate and must not exceed what is necessary to achieve legitimate aims.
principles.description: No AI system should be used for social scoring or mass surveillance purposes, and AI must not be used to cause harm.

principles.name: Safety and Security
principles.definition: Unwanted harms and vulnerabilities should be avoided throughout the AI system lifecycle.
principles.description: AI actors should ensure AI safety and security through prevention, mitigation, and monitoring of risks and threats.

principles.name: Fairness and Non-Discrimination
principles.definition: AI actors should promote social justice, fairness, and non-discrimination.
principles.description: AI systems should be designed and used to promote fairness and prevent or mitigate discriminatory outcomes.

principles.name: Sustainability
principles.definition: AI technologies should be assessed against their impacts on sustainability.
principles.description: AI systems should support the achievement of current and future sustainable development goals.

principles.name: Right to Privacy and Data Protection
principles.definition: Privacy and data protection must be respected, protected, and promoted throughout the AI lifecycle.
principles.description: AI actors should ensure that personal data is collected, used, shared, archived, and deleted in compliance with privacy and data protection frameworks.

principles.name: Human Oversight and Determination
principles.definition: Member States should ensure that AI systems do not displace ultimate human responsibility and accountability.
principles.description: Ethical responsibility and legal liability must remain with humans. AI systems should not be given legal personality.

principles.name: Transparency and Explainability
principles.definition: The transparency and explainability of AI systems should be ensured to the extent appropriate.
principles.description: AI actors should provide meaningful transparency about AI systems, including explainability of processes and decisions.

principles.name: Responsibility and Accountability
principles.definition: AI actors and member states should respect, protect, and promote accountability throughout AI lifecycle.
principles.description: Mechanisms should exist to ensure accountability including auditability, traceability, and human oversight.

principles.name: Awareness and Literacy
principles.definition: Public awareness and understanding of AI should be promoted.
principles.description: AI actors should promote broad awareness and literacy about AI among the public and affected communities.
keyElements: Understanding of AI capabilities and limitations

principles.name: Multi-Stakeholder and Adaptive Governance
principles.definition: International law and national sovereignty should be respected in AI governance.
principles.description: Participation of diverse stakeholders in AI governance processes, with adaptive regulatory frameworks that evolve with technology.
